content.text: An N-gram language model estimates the probability of a sequence of tokens by decomposing the joint probability into a product of conditional probabilities and approximating each conditional using only the immediately preceding N−1 tokens. The name comes from the size of the token window: a unigram considers single tokens in isolation, a bigram considers pairs, a trigram considers triples, and so on. N-gram models were the dominant approach to statistical language modelling from the 1980s through the mid-2010s, powering machine translation, speech recognition, and spell-checkers before neural language models surpassed them.

Despite their simplicity, N-gram models encode a genuine and useful statistical regularity: in natural language, knowing the last one or two words dramatically reduces uncertainty about the next word. The phrase "New York" is far more likely to be followed by "City" than "elephant", and a trigram model captures this. The approximation is weak over long distances — it cannot model subject-verb agreement across a clause boundary, for example — but it is surprisingly effective at short range and requires no parameters beyond the counts of token sequences in a corpus.

N-gram models provide the clearest possible view of the core problem in language modelling: how to estimate P(wₜ | context) from a finite corpus. Every technique in the field — smoothing, back-off, interpolation, neural embeddings — exists to solve the problems that bare N-gram models expose. Understanding N-grams is therefore prerequisite knowledge for understanding why neural language models are designed the way they are.

Practically, N-gram models remain valuable as fast scoring functions, as baselines for evaluating neural models, and in constrained environments (edge devices, real-time spell correction) where neural inference is too slow or too expensive. Kneser-Ney 5-gram models built on Common Crawl are still used as rescoring components in hybrid ASR systems, where they can be queried in microseconds per hypothesis.
Any joint distribution over a sequence of tokens can be written exactly as a product of conditional distributions using the chain rule of probability:

attrs.latex: P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1})

content.text: This is always exact — no approximation yet. The challenge is that conditioning on the full history w₁:t₋₁ requires tracking exponentially many contexts. N-gram models tame this by truncating the history to at most n−1 previous tokens.
An N-gram model applies the (n−1)th-order Markov assumption to the conditional probabilities:

attrs.latex: P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-n+1}, \ldots, w_{t-1})

content.text: P(wₜ) — no context; token probabilities are independent.
P(wₜ | wₜ₋₁) — depends only on the immediately preceding word.
Rare in practice — data sparsity grows exponentially with n.
Given a training corpus, the MLE estimate for a bigram probability is simply the relative frequency of the word pair:

where C(·) is the count function. For a trigram the numerator is C(wₜ₋₂, wₜ₋₁, wₜ) and the denominator is C(wₜ₋₂, wₜ₋₁). These counts are tabulated directly from the corpus — no gradient descent required.

from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import math

def build_bigram_model(sentences):
    """
    sentences: list of lists of strings (pre-tokenised)
    Returns: dict of dicts: probs[w_prev][w] = P(w | w_prev)
    """
    unigram_counts = Counter()
    bigram_counts  = defaultdict(Counter)

    for sent in sentences:
        tokens = ["<s>"] + sent + ["</s>"]
        for i, tok in enumerate(tokens):
            unigram_counts[tok] += 1
            if i > 0:
                bigram_counts[tokens[i-1]][tok] += 1

    probs = {}
    for prev, next_counts in bigram_counts.items():
        total = unigram_counts[prev]
        probs[prev] = {w: c / total for w, c in next_counts.items()}
    return probs

corpus = [
    ["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat"],
    ["the", "cat", "ate", "the", "rat"],
    ["a", "rat", "sat", "on", "a", "mat"],
]
model = build_bigram_model(corpus)
print(f"P(cat | the)  = {model['the']['cat']:.3f}")   # 0.400
print(f"P(mat | the)  = {model['the']['mat']:.3f}")   # 0.200
print(f"P(rat | the)  = {model['the'].get('rat',0):.3f}")  # 0.200

MLE assigns zero probability to any N-gram not seen in training. Zero probability for any single token in a sequence drives the entire sequence probability to zero — the model cannot score any sentence containing an unseen bigram. This is called the
. A vocabulary of size V has V² possible bigrams; real corpora cover only a small fraction of them.
Smoothing redistributes some probability mass from seen N-grams to unseen ones. The most widely used methods:
Pretend every N-gram was seen k extra times
Use lower-order model when count is zero
Fall back to bigram if trigram unseen
State of the art for N-gram LMs; best perplexity
Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts a held-out test set. It is the exponentiated average negative log-probability per token:

Lower perplexity is better. A perplexity of K means the model is, on average, as uncertain as if it had to choose uniformly among K options at each step. A uniform distribution over a 10 000-word vocabulary has perplexity 10 000; a good trigram language model might reach 60–100 on news text.

N-gram models have fixed, bounded context windows (n−1 tokens), cannot share statistical strength across similar words ("dog" and "canine" are entirely separate vocabulary entries), and require storing all N-gram counts — for a 5-gram on a large web corpus this can reach terabytes. Neural language models overcome all three: they have learnable context windows (or, in Transformers, the full sequence), they share representations via word embeddings, and their parameters are O(vocabulary × hidden size), independent of corpus size.

Despite these limitations, N-gram models remain useful as fast baselines, for scoring in beam search, and as components in interpolated systems. Kneser-Ney 5-gram models built on large corpora still achieve competitive perplexity on standard benchmarks compared to small neural language models.
Parameters are corpus counts; building the model is a single pass over the data.
Scoring a token requires a single hash table lookup, making N-gram models orders of magnitude faster than neural alternatives.
Every probability is directly traceable to corpus counts; you can inspect exactly why a particular transition was assigned its probability.
Works for any sequence of discrete tokens — words, characters, subwords, phonemes, MIDI notes — without modification.
New training data can be incorporated by updating counts without retraining from scratch.
A trigram cannot use information from more than two tokens ago. Long-range dependencies (subject-verb agreement, pronoun coreference) are invisible to the model.
A vocabulary of V has V^n possible N-grams. For V=50,000 and n=5, that is 3×10²³ possible 5-grams, of which only a tiny fraction appear in any corpus.
"cat" and "feline" are entirely separate vocabulary entries. A bigram model that has never seen "a feline ran" cannot infer anything from having seen "a cat ran".
Storing a 5-gram model on a web-scale corpus (hundreds of billions of tokens) requires terabytes of storage, even with efficient compression.
Getting good performance requires careful choice among several smoothing methods, each with hyperparameters that must be tuned on held-out data.
Real-World Example: Building a Text Autocomplete System

A trigram model is a practical foundation for keyboard autocomplete. The model is trained on a corpus of the user's past messages; at inference time, given the last two typed words, it suggests the most probable next word. This is exactly the application for which N-gram models were commercialised — the SwiftKey keyboard (2010) was among the first mass-market products to use this approach.

from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import math, heapq

def build_trigram_model(sentences, k=0.1):
    """Build a Katz-smoothed trigram model from a list of token lists."""
    bigram_counts  = defaultdict(Counter)
    trigram_counts = defaultdict(Counter)
    unigram_counts = Counter()
    vocab = set()

    for sent in sentences:
        tokens = ["<s>", "<s>"] + sent + ["</s>"]
        for i, tok in enumerate(tokens):
            unigram_counts[tok] += 1
            vocab.add(tok)
            if i >= 1:
                bigram_counts[tokens[i-1]][tok] += 1
            if i >= 2:
                trigram_counts[(tokens[i-2], tokens[i-1])][tok] += 1

    V = len(vocab)

    def trigram_prob(w1, w2, w3):
        context = (w1, w2)
        num = trigram_counts[context][w3] + k
        den = sum(trigram_counts[context].values()) + k * V
        if den == 0:
            # Fall back to bigram
            num = bigram_counts[w2][w3] + k
            den = unigram_counts[w2] + k * V
        return num / den

    return trigram_prob, vocab

def top_k_completions(w1, w2, model, vocab, k=5):
    """Return top-k most probable next tokens given the last two words."""
    scores = [(model(w1, w2, w), w) for w in vocab if w not in ("<s>",)]
    return heapq.nlargest(k, scores)

# --- Example ---
corpus = [
    "the cat sat on the mat".split(),
    "the cat ate the rat".split(),
    "a rat sat on a mat".split(),
    "the dog sat on the floor".split(),
    "the dog ate the bone".split(),
    "a dog ran on the floor".split(),
]

model, vocab = build_trigram_model(corpus)
completions = top_k_completions("the", "cat", model, vocab)
print("After 'the cat':")
for prob, word in completions:
    print(f"  {word:10s}  P={prob:.4f}")
# After 'the cat':
#   sat         P=0.4286
#   ate         P=0.4286
#   </s>        P=0.0714
Mistake 1: Evaluating perplexity on training data

Training perplexity for a smoothed model is not a meaningful number — it conflates smoothing quality with generalisation. Always evaluate perplexity on a held-out test set that was not used to choose smoothing hyperparameters.

# WRONG — no sentence boundaries; trigrams span across sentence ends
corpus_flat = " ".join(" ".join(sent) for sent in sentences).split()
# This creates spurious bigrams like ["</s>", "<s>"] treated as real context

# RIGHT — wrap each sentence independently
for sent in sentences:
    tokens = ["<s>", "<s>"] + sent + ["</s>"]
    for i in range(2, len(tokens)):
        trigram_counts[(tokens[i-2], tokens[i-1])][tokens[i]] += 1
Mistake 3: Using add-1 (Laplace) smoothing on large vocabularies

Laplace smoothing (k=1) transfers too much probability mass to unseen N-grams when the vocabulary is large. With V=50,000, a bigram that occurred once receives the same adjustment as one that never occurred, while seen bigrams lose a huge fraction of their probability. Use add-k with small k (0.001–0.1) or, better, Kneser-Ney smoothing, which is specifically designed to avoid this problem.
It consistently outperforms add-k, Katz, and linear interpolation on held-out perplexity, particularly for higher-order N-grams.
Use <s> and </s> tokens so the model learns start-of-sentence and end-of-sentence distributions rather than treating all positions equivalently.
Choose n=3 or n=5 depending on corpus size.
Trigrams are a good default for corpora under 100M tokens; 5-grams benefit from web-scale data but hurt on small corpora due to sparsity.
The KenLM library implements Kneser-Ney N-gram models with ARPA format output, memory-mapped queries, and quantisation, making it practical for large-scale deployment.
A model trained on news text will have poor perplexity on social media — always evaluate on data from the same distribution as your deployment.

Building an N-gram model requires a single O(CT) pass over the corpus (C = corpus size in tokens, T = n-gram length), then storing the counts in a hash table. Query time is O(1) per token — a single hash lookup. This makes N-gram scoring 100–1000x faster than a forward pass through a neural language model.

The bottleneck is memory: storing all N-gram counts for a web-scale corpus requires hundreds of gigabytes. KenLM addresses this with: (1) sorting N-grams to enable binary search instead of a hash table, (2) storing counts as 8-bit quantised values, and (3) using PROBING or TRIE data structures to reduce memory by 3–5x versus a naive hash map. A 5-gram model on 1 trillion tokens (Google n-gram corpus) requires roughly 100 GB in KenLM's quantised format versus several terabytes for a plain hash map.

# Training perplexity vs n
import math

def perplexity_of_model(model_fn, test_sents, order):
    """Compute test perplexity for an N-gram model."""
    total_log_prob = 0.0
    total_tokens   = 0
    for sent in test_sents:
        tokens = ["<s>"] * (order - 1) + sent + ["</s>"]
        for i in range(order - 1, len(tokens)):
            context = tuple(tokens[i - order + 1: i])
            p = model_fn(*context, tokens[i])
            total_log_prob += math.log(p + 1e-300)
            total_tokens   += 1
    return math.exp(-total_log_prob / total_tokens)

# Rule of thumb: perplexity falls as n increases (up to data limits)
# n=1 (unigram)  →  PPL ~= |V| (vocabulary size)
# n=2 (bigram)   →  PPL ~= 500–2000 on news text
# n=3 (trigram)  →  PPL ~= 60–200 on news text
# n=5 (5-gram)   →  PPL ~= 50–150 on news text (diminishing returns)
# Neural LM      →  PPL ~= 20–50 on news text
— The de-facto standard for production N-gram models. Implements modified Kneser-Ney, outputs ARPA format, and provides a Python wrapper (pip install kenlm). Scales to web-sized corpora.
— nltk.lm provides MLE, Laplace, and KneserNey language model implementations with a unified API. Convenient for educational use and small corpora.
— The original industrial-strength language modelling toolkit (Stanford). Supports dozens of smoothing methods and is still used in ASR pipelines.
— Neural MT frameworks that use KenLM N-gram models for rescoring beam search hypotheses, combining neural and N-gram strengths.

# NLTK KneserNey example
from nltk.lm import KneserNeyInterpolated
from nltk.lm.preprocessing import padded_everygram_pipeline
from nltk.util import bigrams

sentences = [
    "the cat sat on the mat".split(),
    "the cat ate the rat".split(),
    "a dog ran on the mat".split(),
]

# Build padded N-gram sequences
n = 3
train_data, padded_vocab = padded_everygram_pipeline(n, sentences)
model = KneserNeyInterpolated(n)
model.fit(train_data, padded_vocab)

# Score a trigram
print(model.score("sat", ["the", "cat"]))  # P(sat | the, cat)
print(model.perplexity(["the cat sat".split()]))
ASR rescoring, small gains over neural alone

N-gram models are purely statistical summaries of the training corpus and have no explicit filtering mechanisms. A model trained on unfiltered internet text will assign high probability to harmful sequences that appear frequently in the corpus — profanity, personal information patterns, or toxic phrases. The model cannot reason about content; it simply reflects corpus frequencies.

A reliability concern specific to N-gram models is vocabulary coverage: any token not in the training vocabulary requires a special UNK handling strategy. If UNK is a single token, all unknown words are treated as identical — the model cannot distinguish between a rare technical term and a user-typed name. For production deployments, maintain an explicit vocabulary list, assign a small fixed probability to UNK, and monitor the OOV rate on incoming queries as a signal of distribution shift.
N-gram models apply the (n−1)th-order Markov assumption: P(wₜ | w₁:ₜ₋₁) ≈ P(wₜ | wₜ₋ₙ₊₁:ₜ₋₁). This converts the exponential joint distribution into a table of local conditional probabilities.
MLE estimates are raw corpus counts: P̂(wₜ | wₜ₋₁) = C(wₜ₋₁, wₜ) / C(wₜ₋₁). Zero counts for unseen N-grams are the central problem.
Smoothing methods (Laplace, Katz back-off, interpolation, Kneser-Ney) redistribute probability mass from seen to unseen N-grams. Kneser-Ney is the state of the art.
Perplexity = exp(−(1/T) Σ log P(wₜ | context)) measures how well the model predicts held-out text; lower is better.
N-gram models are 100–1000x faster at inference than neural language models and require no GPU, making them valuable in latency-critical or resource-constrained settings.
Key limitations: fixed bounded context, no cross-word generalisation, data sparsity growing as V^n, and terabyte-scale storage for web corpora.
Use KenLM for production N-gram models; NLTK's lm module for educational and small-scale use.

Understanding N-grams is foundational: every design choice in neural language models (context windows, embeddings, smoothing via weight decay) can be understood as solving a problem that N-gram models expose.
